content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Commissioners Houston, Japan, McAndrews,
     Metzger and Quinn
                                                    Date : May 22, 1980


                                                    M-80-135

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Sarah Cameron

Subject: Standards for flexibility in use of regulation 18730--
          the standardized Conflict of Interest Code--for agencies
          for which the FPPC is the code reviewing body

     At the time regulation 18730 (the standardized boiler-
plate code) was adopted, the Commission indicated that it
wished to provide flexibility from the standardized provi-
sions in certain circumstances. The staff was directed to
consider circumstances in which deviations from the
standardized boilerplate would be appropriate. This
memorandum sets forth the general types of considerations
which should motivate the Commission in determining whether
the codes of individual agencies should be permitted to
deviate from the standardized code. In general, we think
deviation should be both permitted and encouraged in situa-
tions where a failure to deviate from the standardized
code would mean that foreseeable conflicts of interest
would not be disclosed. However, when an agency cannot
demonstrate a need for deviation, we think the Commission
should require incorporation of the standardized code.
We have also attached a chart which generally outlines
the neglect which most agencies have demonstrated toward
regularly updating their codes.

     The primary need for flexibility in the use of
regulation 18730 is to accommodate agencies which, in
order to assure that foreseeable conflicts of interest
are disclosed, must go beyond the definitions set forth
in the Act and the general provisions required to be
contained in conflict of interest codes. For example,
the code for the Public Employees Retirement System
expressly covers all business entities, regardless of
whether they are doing business in California, if they
may be affected by the investment policy of the System.
Obviously, in the application of regulation 18730, we
would want to continue to permit inclusion of such a
provision. To do otherwise clearly would undercut a
major purpose of the agency's conflict of interest code.

Commissioners
May 22, 1980
Page Two


     Similarly, the code for the Air Resources Board, the
Department of Corporations and a number of health agencies
treat certain non-profit organizations as "business entities."
The Commission permitted this departure from the normal defi-
nition of business entity contained in Section 82005 because
of the substantial likelihood that the activities of these
departments could affect such entities.

     Another departure from the normal conflict of interest
code provisions involves the disclosure requirements for
certain Franchise Tax Board and Board of Equalization auditors.
Rather than disclosing certain types of financial interests,
this restricted class of auditors is merely required to sign
a declaration that they have not worked on any matter involving
any of their financial interests. This special approach to
disclosure was created by the Commission for several reasons:
disclosure could not otherwise be tailored; due to the large
pool of business entities from which an auditor's assignments
were chosen, it was very unlikely that an auditor would ever
be assigned to audit one of his interests; and, auditors sub-
ject to this special disclosure provision were not at the
policy making level.

     The point of these examples is that significant variations
from the requirements of conflict of interest codes have not
been granted by the Commission unless the agency could show
special circumstances. These special circumstances still can
be accommodated even if the Commission generally requires that
agencies incorporate regulation 18730.

     Since the regulation will be incorporated, an agency's
code probably will consist of one page in addition to the
appendices which contain the designated employees and the
disclosure categories. Attached is a model for this one-page
code. In order to accommodate unique provisions such as those
discussed above, we need only state that "Except as otherwise
provided in the code, the terms of regulation 18730 are incor-
porated." We can then specify in one or two additional para-
graphs those terms or provisions which have been modified to
satisfy the needs of that particular agency.

     A totally different question is whether any agency, whether
or not it has special circumstances which justify a substantive
departure from various provisions of the Act, should be able to
avoid incorporation of the regulation altogether. I do not see
any reason why this should be allowed. Two of the purposes of
standardization are to facilitate the amendment of codes in
order that they shall be kept up to date and to make the provi-
sions of the code as uniform as possible so that the advice
and enforcement processes can be handled more efficiently by

Commissioners
May 22, 1980
Page Three

eliminating the need to study each code to determine what it
says. Both of these purposes are undercut by permitting agen-
cies to opt out of standardization.

However, if the Commission believes that agencies should
be able to keep their old codes, then this should only be
allowed if the agency has a track record of keeping its code
up to date without such amendments being initiated by the FPPC.
In other words, only if an agency has in the past updated its
code on a regular basis to reflect amendments in the Act or in
our regulations (amendments about which we inform agencies
specifically in our annual newsletter), should it be able to
keep its old code instead of incorporating the regulations by
reference. Consequently, if you decide to allow agencies to
keep their old codes, it should be made explicitly clear that
if they fail to keep them up to date in the future, they will
be required to incorporate the regulation.

Finally, the standardized code contains a uniform filing
date for annual statements--April 1--and even if an agency is
allowed to keep its old code, use of this standard filing date
should perhaps be required as a condition. This would allow us
to simplify administration of the statements both by us and by
the agency filing officer. Our technical assistance personnel,
for example, would be able to tell filing officers that state-
ments were late if they were not received by April 1 rather than
trying to distinguish between standardized and non-standardized
codes and referring people under the latter to their codes to
determine the filing deadline.

In conclusion, I recommend that the regulation be incor-
porated by all agencies for which the FPPC is the code reviewing
body. Departures from the regulation can be accommodated in
the short code which accompanies the appendix. Such departures
should be allowed when necessary to assure that all foreseeable
conflict of interest situations are disclosed or governed by
the code. Other departures, such as the unique disclosure made
by auditors, may be justified because the usual application of
the regulation would have an unduly harsh result, but this kind
of reasoning should be applied in only a limited number of
cases. Finally, if you believe there is some justification for
allowing agencies to avoid the regulation altogether, you may
still want to allow this only in cases in which the agency has
a demonstrated track record of updating its code after receiving
our information concerning changes in the Act and in our
regulations.

The chart which is attached shows, by types of agencies
and year of first effective date of the code, how many agencies
have formally amended their codes, including how many have

Commissioners
May 22, 1980
Page Four


amended them more than once. 1980 is included simply to fill
out the picture of the number of codes adopted. In addition,
22 agencies have non-substantive alterations in effect in their
codes on an interim basis pursuant to regulation 18752.


STC:cjb
Attachments

AMENDMENT OF CODES

Type of Agency

Effective Date          Not      Amended      Amended More
of Original Code        Total    Amended      Once          Than Once

State Agencies

1976                     2        1            1             -
1977                    48       44            3             1
1978                    34       32            2             -
1979                    17       17            -             -
1980                     5        -            -             -

Water Districts

1976                     9        7            1             1
1977                    36       35            -            1*
1978                     1        1            -             -
1979                     2        2            -             -
1980                     2        2            -             -

Irrigation Districts

1977                    24       22            2             -
1979                     2        2            -             -

Reclamation Districts

1977                    11       11            -             -
1979                     1        1            -             -

School Districts

1977                    59       59            -             -
1978                    12       12            -             -
1979                    20       20            -             -
1980                     3        3            -             -

Community College
Districts

1977                     2        1            1             -
1978                    31       30            1             -
1979                     1        1            -             -

Other Special Districts

1977                     2        2            -             -
1978                    28       25           3**            -
1979                     8        8            -             -

*Amended twice in 1980.
**One both adopted and amended in 1978.

AMENDMENT OF CODES
Page 2


Type of Agency

Effective Date          Not        Amended      Amended More
of Original Code      Total      Amended      Once         Than Once

Joint Powers Agencies

1979                    14          14           -            -
1980                    17          17           -            -

The Political Reform Act, Government Code

Sections 81000, et seq., requires state and local government

agencies to adopt and promulgate Conflict of Interest Codes.

The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted a regulation,

2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730, which contains the terms of a

standard model Conflict of Interest Code, which can be incorporated

by reference, and which may be amended by the Fair Political

Practices Commission to conform to amendments in the Political

Reform Act after public notice and hearings. Therefore, the

terms of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730 and any amendments to

it duly adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission

along with the attached Appendix in which officials and employees

are designated and disclosure categories are set forth, are

hereby incorporated by reference and constitute the Conflict

of Interest Code of the Fair Political Practices Commission.

Designated employees shall file statements of economic interests

pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18730(b)(4)(A).

sections.question_synthetic: Should the FPPC require all agencies to incorporate the standardized conflict of interest code (regulation 18730), and if so, under what circumstances should deviations be permitted?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC should require all agencies to incorporate regulation 18730 by reference, with deviations permitted only when necessary to ensure foreseeable conflicts of interest are disclosed. Agencies with poor track records of updating codes should be required to use the standardized version with a uniform April 1 filing date.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal FPPC staff memorandum to Commissioners rather than a traditional advice letter response to external inquiry. No traditional Q/A sections but contains clear policy recommendation and conclusion. Document includes analytical chart of code amendment history by agency type.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should the FPPC require all agencies to incorporate the standardized conflict of interest code (regulation 18730), and if so, under what circumstances should deviations be permitted?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC should require all agencies to incorporate regulation 18730 by reference, with deviations permitted only when necessary to ensure foreseeable conflicts of interest are disclosed. Agencies with poor track records of updating codes should be required to use the standardized version with a uniform April 1 filing date.

embedding.summary: Staff memorandum to FPPC Commissioners recommending mandatory incorporation of the standardized conflict of interest code (regulation 18730) by all agencies with FPPC oversight, while allowing case-specific deviations for agencies with demonstrated special circumstances that require disclosure beyond standard provisions.
